quiz.title: Unit 3 Quiz 8, Grade 11 Biology
quiz.subtitle: Biochemistry: Carbohydrates Deep Dive
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Monosaccharides, Disaccharides, Polysaccharides, Digestion, Energy Storage

questions.question: What is the main component of cell membranes?
questions.explanation: Phospholipids form the lipid bilayer of cell membranes. They have hydrophilic heads and hydrophobic tails that self-assemble into membranes. Cholesterol modulates fluidity, but phospholipids are the primary structural component.

questions.question: A molecule comprised of two amino acids is called a(n):
questions.explanation: Two amino acids joined by a peptide bond form a dipeptide. Disaccharides are carbohydrates, polypeptides are longer chains, and proteins are functional folded polypeptides.

questions.question: What is the general chemical formula for carbohydrates?
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates are named for carbon plus water, following the general formula (CH₂O)n where n is typically 3-7 for simple sugars.

questions.question: Which molecule is a monosaccharide serving as universal energy currency?
questions.explanation: Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) is the primary monosaccharide fuel for cellular respiration. Sucrose is a disaccharide; starch and glycogen are polysaccharides.

questions.question: What two monosaccharides form sucrose?
questions.explanation: Sucrose (table sugar) is formed from glucose and fructose linked by an α-1,2-glycosidic bond. It is the primary transport sugar in plants.

questions.question: What two monosaccharides form lactose?
questions.explanation: Lactose (milk sugar) consists of glucose and galactose linked by a β-1,4-glycosidic bond. It is found only in mammalian milk.

questions.question: What type of bond links monosaccharides together?
questions.explanation: Glycosidic bonds are covalent bonds formed between monosaccharides through dehydration synthesis, releasing water.

questions.question: What is the primary function of starch?
questions.explanation: Starch consists of amylose and amylopectin. It is the primary energy storage polymer in plants, stored in chloroplasts and amyloplasts.

questions.question: What is the primary function of glycogen?
questions.explanation: Glycogen is highly branched and stored in liver and muscles. It allows rapid glucose mobilization during energy demand in animals.

questions.question: What is the primary function of cellulose?
questions.explanation: Cellulose has β-1,4-glycosidic bonds creating straight chains that form strong microfibrils. It provides tensile strength to plant cell walls.

questions.question: Where is chitin primarily found?
questions.explanation: Chitin contains nitrogen (N-acetylglucosamine) and forms exoskeletons of insects/crustaceans and cell walls of fungi.

questions.question: How much energy do carbohydrates provide per gram?
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates yield approximately 4 kcal/g (16.7 kJ/g) upon oxidation. Lipids yield more (~9 kcal/g) due to higher C-H bond density.

questions.question: Which enzyme initiates starch digestion in the mouth?
questions.explanation: Salivary amylase breaks α-1,4-glycosidic bonds in starch. It is inactivated by stomach acid but resumes in the small intestine.

questions.question: Where are disaccharidases located?
questions.explanation: Enzymes like maltase, sucrase, and lactase are embedded in intestinal cell membranes (brush border) to hydrolyze disaccharides before absorption.

questions.question: What causes lactose intolerance?
questions.explanation: Lactose intolerance results from decreased lactase production after childhood, preventing hydrolysis of the β-1,4 bond in lactose.

questions.question: What is the approximate total glycogen storage capacity in humans?
questions.explanation: The body stores ~100g in the liver and ~400g in muscles. Excess carbohydrates beyond this limit are converted to fat.

questions.question: Which monosaccharide is structurally similar to glucose but differs in the arrangement of atoms?
questions.explanation: Galactose is an epimer of glucose, differing only in the orientation of the hydroxyl group on carbon 4. Maltose and sucrose are disaccharides; starch is a polysaccharide.

questions.question: Which sugar is the sweetest naturally occurring monosaccharide?
questions.explanation: Fructose is the sweetest naturally occurring monosaccharide, about 1.7 times sweeter than sucrose. Glucose and galactose are less sweet; maltose is a disaccharide.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered carbohydrate chemistry and digestion.
scoreMessages.70: Good job. Review disaccharide composition and polysaccharide functions.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on monosaccharide types and glycosidic bonds.
scoreMessages.0: Please review carbohydrate structure, function, and digestion carefully.
